sections.content: GMDSS divides the world's oceans into four Sea Areas, labelled A1 through A4. Each area is defined by the type of radio or satellite coverage available, and the equipment a vessel must carry depends on which Sea Areas it operates in. This classification ensures that every vessel has the right communication tools for the coverage zone it sails through.

rows: A1
Within range of at least one VHF coast station providing continuous DSC alerting coverage
Typically 20–50 nautical miles from shore, depending on antenna height and local geography
A2
Within range of at least one MF coast station providing continuous DSC alerting coverage, excluding Sea Area A1
Up to approximately 150–400 nautical miles from shore, beyond A1 coverage
A3
Within the Inmarsat geostationary satellite coverage area, excluding Sea Areas A1 and A2
Approximately 70°N to 70°S latitude — covers most of the world's oceans
A4
All remaining sea areas outside A1, A2, and A3
Polar regions above approximately 70°N and below approximately 70°S

sections.content: The Sea Areas are nested: A1 is closest to shore, A2 extends further out, A3 covers the broad mid-ocean belt under Inmarsat satellites, and A4 captures the polar extremes. A vessel sailing from port out into the open ocean might pass through A1, then A2, then A3 in succession. The further from shore, the more sophisticated (and expensive) the communication equipment required.

sections.title: Why Sea Areas Matter for Certification
sections.content: The Restricted Operator Certificate (ROC) qualifies you to operate GMDSS equipment in Sea Area A1 only. To operate in Sea Areas A2, A3, or A4, you need the General Operator Certificate (GOC). Most coastal and short-sea vessels operate exclusively within A1.

sections.prompt: A vessel is 200 nautical miles from the nearest coast, outside VHF coast station range but within MF coast station DSC coverage. Which Sea Area is it operating in?
sections.explanation: Sea Area A2 is defined as the area within MF coast station DSC range but outside A1 (VHF coast station range). At 200 NM from shore, the vessel is well beyond the typical 20–50 NM VHF range but within MF coverage.

sections.content: Vessels operating exclusively in Sea Area A1 must carry a specific set of GMDSS equipment. This equipment list is designed to meet all nine functional requirements using VHF-range terrestrial communications and satellite-based positioning/alerting.

rows: VHF radio with DSC (Ch 70)
Distress alerting, safety, and general communications
Must be capable of transmitting and receiving DSC on Channel 70 (156.525 MHz) and voice on Ch 16 and other channels
NAVTEX receiver
Receiving Maritime Safety Information
Automatically receives and prints navigational warnings, weather forecasts, and SAR information on 518 kHz (English) and 490 kHz (local language)
406 MHz EPIRB
Satellite distress alerting and position indication
Float-free, automatically activated Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon; detected by Cospas-Sarsat satellites; registered to the vessel with encoded MMSI
2 x SART or AIS-SART
Locating signals for search and rescue
Radar SART (9 GHz) or AIS-SART; carried in survival craft to help SAR units home in on survivors
Survival craft VHF radio
On-scene communications from lifeboats/liferafts
Portable, waterproof VHF transceiver for use after abandoning ship; at least 2 required on cargo ships, 3 on passenger ships

sections.title: SOLAS vs Non-SOLAS Vessels
sections.content: The equipment table above applies to SOLAS-compulsory vessels (cargo ships ≥300 GT on international voyages, all passenger ships). Small craft and pleasure boats are generally not SOLAS vessels and are not legally required to carry NAVTEX receivers, SARTs, or EPIRBs — though all of these are strongly recommended. National regulations may impose additional requirements for non-SOLAS vessels.

sections.title: EPIRB Registration
sections.content: A 406 MHz EPIRB must be properly registered with the national authority and encoded with the vessel's MMSI or other unique identifier. An unregistered EPIRB delays rescue because the RCC cannot identify the vessel or confirm whether the alert is genuine.

sections.prompt: The dedicated DSC distress and safety calling channel on VHF is Channel ___.
sections.explanation: VHF Channel 70 (156.525 MHz) is reserved exclusively for Digital Selective Calling. No voice traffic is permitted on this channel. All GMDSS-equipped VHF radios must maintain a continuous watch on Ch 70.

sections.content: In addition to the mandatory equipment, A1 vessels must maintain a continuous watch on both VHF Channel 16 (156.800 MHz) for voice distress and safety traffic and VHF Channel 70 for DSC alerts. Modern VHF radios with built-in DSC capability handle the Ch 70 watch automatically using a dedicated receiver, so the operator can use the main receiver on other working channels while the DSC watch runs in the background.

sections.prompt: Which of the following is NOT part of the mandatory A1 GMDSS equipment fit?

options.text: MF/HF radio with DSC on 2187.5 kHz

sections.explanation: MF/HF radio with DSC is required for Sea Areas A2 and beyond. Sea Area A1 vessels need only VHF radio with DSC. The 406 MHz EPIRB and NAVTEX receiver are both mandatory for A1.

sections.content: It is worth noting that vessels may voluntarily carry equipment above the A1 minimum — for example, an Inmarsat terminal for commercial communications — but the GMDSS carriage requirement is based on the Sea Area in which the vessel is certified to operate. Flag state administrations may also impose additional national requirements beyond the SOLAS minimum.

points: GMDSS defines four Sea Areas (A1–A4) based on available radio and satellite coverage, from coastal VHF range out to the polar regions.
Sea Area A1 covers the area within range of a VHF coast station with DSC capability, typically 20–50 nautical miles from shore.
Mandatory A1 equipment includes: VHF radio with DSC (Ch 70), NAVTEX receiver, 406 MHz EPIRB, two SARTs or AIS-SARTs, and survival craft VHF radios.
The ROC certificate covers Sea Area A1 only; the GOC is required for operations in A2, A3, or A4.
Continuous watch must be maintained on VHF Channel 16 (voice) and Channel 70 (DSC).
